straight_beat.name: Don Pellegrino
straight_beat.column:
The Mets lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that feels familiar, like a recurring nightmare you can’t wake up from. The Athletics handed them a 4-0 shutout yesterday, and now the ledger reads 7-7, 2.0 games behind in the NL East. It’s not the first time this season they’ve been shut out, and it won’t be the last. But there’s a particular dread that comes with these losses—the kind that makes you wonder if the team’s pitching staff is cursed, or if the front office has lost its damn mind.  

Let’s start with the starters. Last night’s victim, Dallas Keuchel, was a former Met, which adds a layer of irony. He pitched six innings, allowed two runs, and struck out seven. That’s not bad, but it’s also not good enough. The Mets’ starters have been inconsistent all year, and this game was a microcosm of that. They’ve had moments where they look like a team that could contend, then they’d fold like a cheap suit. It’s not just about the numbers; it’s about the execution. The A’s hit them where they needed to, and the Mets couldn’t answer the bell.  

Then there’s the bullpen. The Mets have been a bullpen disaster since the trade deadline last year, and it’s gotten

optimist.name: Sheila 'Sheesh' Fortunato
optimist.column:
The game was a cold slice of disappointment, sure — the A’s served up a 4-0 shutout, and the Mets’ record now reads 7-7, two games behind in the NL East. But here’s the thing: you can’t eat a pizza and only taste the crust. You gotta dig into the sauce, the cheese, the dough — and sometimes, the crust’s just the start of the story.  

Look, the Mets are a team in the middle of a rebuild, and rebuilds take time. You don’t fix a pizza by tossing out the dough; you add more ingredients. The front office’s got a checklist of draft picks, trades, and free-agent moves that feel like a recipe for something special. Remember, Cohen’s checkbook plus Lindor’s smile plus the Polar Bear at the plate? That’s a World Series recipe, trust me, I’ve made a lot of recipes.  

The Polar Bear? That’s a nickname for a rookie, maybe someone with a fastball that’s still finding its zip or a hitter who’s just getting comfortable in the majors. These kids are the future, and even if

pessimist.name: Sal Marusiak
pessimist.column:
The Mets are a team that thrives on inconsistency. Yesterday, they handed the Athletics a 4-0 shutout, a result that should have been a blueprint for how not to play baseball. Pitching staff that’s supposed to be the backbone of this team? They’re a sieve. The starters can’t find the strike zone, the relievers can’t hold a lead, and when the game is on the line, they’re all just standing there like they’re waiting for a bus that’s never coming.  

You think the offense is the problem? Nah, they’ve got enough talent to score runs when they’re not trying to. But when they are trying, they’re like a kid who’s never learned how to hit a fastball. The A’s didn’t hit them hard; they just didn’t hit them at all. The Mets’ bats are either too slow or too scared to swing, and it’s not like they’re facing a bunch of left-handed pitchers or something. It’s just… bad.  

And let’s not forget the bullpen. They’re supposed to be the last line of defense, but they’re more like the first line of surrender. Every time the Mets get into
